deconstruction_targets: the inversion of subject and object in the process of production
the obscuration of capital-labour relationships by circulation
the false equivalence between surplus-value and profit
the portrayal of capital as an independent source of surplus-value
summary: The passage deconstructs classical political economy by exposing how circulation distorts the relationship between labor and capital, obscures surplus-value extraction, and enables capitalist self-valorization through market mechanisms. It argues that surplus-value is not generated in production but realized through circulation, where competition and price deviations mask exploitation as mere 'business acumen' or cost-saving. The text condemns the Ricardian school's conflation of profit and surplus-value rates and reveals how capital reifies itself by treating labor-power as a commodity and wages as neutral value-forms. While intensely critical, it offers no constructive alternative—only a theoretical dismantling of existing economic narratives.
